goal: Suggest preprocessing and feature-engineering actions for predicting apartment sale prices from housing rows plus timestamp-aligned macroeconomic lookup data under the Kaggle RMSLE metric.

task_characteristics.analysis_notes: The largest relevant labeled table is train.csv with 30,471 rows; test.csv has 7,662 rows and macro.csv adds 2,484 timestamp-keyed lookup rows. The benchmark-relevant case-insensitive column union across train, test, macro, and sample submission files is exactly 391 columns, driven by a 292-column housing table, a 100-column macro table, and a compact 2-column submission file. The current curated Sberbank candidate bank rewards 22 good preprocessing actions, 8 of them core_preprocessing, so the task remains high-complexity despite its small row count. Benchmark-relevant work centers on impossible-value cleanup for apartment geometry, timestamp parsing and calendar features, timestamp-aligned macro joins, categorical handling, grouped time counts, and housing-specific ratios and interactions.

overview.title: Sberbank Russian Housing Market
overview.summary: Predict Russian apartment sale prices from housing attributes and macroeconomic context.
overview.raw_text: Sberbank Russian Housing Market is a Featured Kaggle real-estate regression competition in which participants predict price_doc for each id in test.csv. The task combines a wide apartment-level housing table with a timestamp-keyed macroeconomic lookup table, so strong solutions typically mix housing-specific data cleaning with selected macro joins and timestamp-derived features. Public notebooks in the local collector consistently treat it as a preprocessing-heavy benchmark with anomaly repair around full_sq, life_sq, kitch_sq, floor, max_floor, build_year, and state, followed by categorical handling, grouped temporal counts, and a small number of housing-specific ratios or interactions. Local competition-selection metadata records Sberbank as the host, Featured as the category, a 25,000 USD prize pool, 3,264 teams, and a June 29, 2017 deadline.

dataset_description.summary: The benchmark-relevant raw bundle is a five-file competition package: train.csv (30,471 x 292), test.csv (7,662 x 291), macro.csv (2,484 x 100), sample_submission.csv (7,662 x 2), and one plain-text data dictionary.
dataset_description.raw_text: The local download bundle contains four CSVs plus data_dictionary.txt. train.csv provides 30,471 labeled transactions with columns such as id, timestamp, full_sq, life_sq, floor, max_floor, material, build_year, num_room, kitch_sq, state, product_type, and target price_doc. test.csv mirrors the same housing schema without the target and contains 7,662 prediction rows. macro.csv provides 2,484 daily macroeconomic snapshots keyed by timestamp and includes indicators such as balance_trade, eurrub, deposits_rate, mortgage_value, mortgage_rate, income_per_cap, and apartment_build that are selectively joined by strong notebook baselines. sample_submission.csv defines the final id-indexed prediction format for price_doc. data_dictionary.txt describes the main housing fields, the macro indicators, and the neighborhood feature prefixes used throughout the raw tables. Across the benchmark-relevant CSVs, the exact case-insensitive raw-column union is 391 columns.
